content.body: A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude (size) and direction. Scalars have magnitude only. Vectors are used in physics (forces, velocity), navigation, computer graphics, and engineering.

Scalar vs Vector:
Scalars: mass, temperature, speed, time, distance
Vectors: force, velocity, displacement, acceleration, weight

Vector notation:
→AB (arrow from A to B)
v or v (bold)
Column vector: (x/y) — top is x-component, bottom is y-component

Magnitude of vector (x/y): |v| = √(x²+y²)

Position Vectors:
If O is the origin, the position vector of point A is OA.

Midpoint: if A has position vector a and B has position vector b,
midpoint M has position vector = (a+b)/2

Vector from A to B: AB = OB − OA = b − a

questions.question: Which of these is a vector quantity?
questions.explanation: Velocity has both magnitude AND direction (e.g. 50 km/h northward). Speed only has magnitude.

questions.question: Which is a scalar?
questions.explanation: Distance is a scalar (just a number of metres with no direction). Displacement includes direction.

questions.question: If a=2b, vectors a and b are:
questions.explanation: If a = 2b, then a is in the same direction as b (just twice as long). They are parallel.
